title: Grand Unified Wiggle Theory
definition: A theoretical framework that posits a continuous, scale-invariant relationship between the fundamental vibrational modes of reality across multiple hierarchical levels.

difficulty_levels.beginner: A model suggesting everything in the universe is made up of tiny wiggles, which come together to create larger structures and patterns, like atoms, cells, planets, and societies.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: A Grand Unified Theory (GUT) that applies String Theory concepts to explain the emergent behavior observed at various scales in nature, from subatomic particles to societal structures.
difficulty_levels.expert: An extension of String Theory positing a nested hierarchy of vibrational modes (wiggles) that give rise to the complexity we observe in nature. The theory highlights the unity and self-similarity between these levels, offering a unified framework for understanding the emergent behavior observed across various scales.

extra_fields.insight: The universe is composed of wiggly things, with everything from subatomic particles to planets being built upon this shared foundation.
